data.title: ULTA Beauty: The International Expansion Plan Is Taking Shape
data.description:
Short Summary of the Past Few Years

It's been a while since I've covered ULTA Beauty (NASDAQ:ULTA), but I've been following this company for a long time now. Back in 2023, I wrote about how ULTA was slowing down domestically in the USA, and that they needed to start looking beyond the USA. I would like to think that they've followed my advice, but the company has indeed started to expand internationally. These initiatives could bring back the growth for ULTA, which is what analysts are looking for.

The uncertainty around the company's long-term future started around 2023 and the stock has under-performed the market in 2024, as ULTA was slow in rolling out these plans for international expansion. However, after some delays, it seems that ULTA is now successfully expanding. As we expect that growth will start to pick up again, we rate the company as a hold due to its increased valuation, but improved growth prospects.

Three Initiatives (Mexico, Middle East, & UK)

At the moment, ULTA is rolling out their international expansion strategy. In summary they are focusing on three main markets: Mexico, the Middle East, and the UK. At the moment, the Mexican expansion is rolling out as planned with a joint venture, this is now starting to contribute to earnings this year.

In the middle east, ULTA has (finally) launched a franchise program, which could deliver high margins with relatively low risk. Franchise programs generally shift most of the risk to the franchisee, and do not require a large capital investment to the franchisor. They are also highly scalable, and could deliver strong growth. Moreover, the Middle East is an attractive market for premium beauty products due to increasing wealth, and partnering with a local operator is smart because they understand the market better.

Finally, ULTA acquired Space NK, operating in the UK and Ireland, expanding with around 80 stores. The UK is a big market that is relatively under-served, Sephora does not operate a lot of stores there yet relative to other markets.

Across these markets, ULTA is using different market entry strategies. I think that this is a good decision by ULTA's management, which is thoughtful in their approach. It seems to me like they are testing the waters and seeing what works. It might have taken them more time, but I feel that these plans are well prepared which is beneficial in the long-run than rushing them through.

Most Recent Earnings

The most recent earnings report from Q2 shows a very impressive 9.3% sales growth. This is driven by both improved comp sales, as well as the immediate impact of the Space NK acquisition, which was doing around 250m in revenue annually and grew 30% in 2024. This shows healthy performance from ULTA.

Capital Allocation

ULTA has a good sense of capital allocation, continuing its share repurchases, and investing back into the business. As some people might have expected, ULTA did not become a dividend stock tied to the US market, and the company still has a lot of potential to grow into a multinational beauty retailer.

FY 2025 and Long Term Outlook

ULTA updated its FY2025 outlook, and improved guidance during the Q2 conference call in August. The international growth plan is contributing to the top-line revenue, and the number of new stores openings is picking up again to 63 new stores this year. That is up from previous years with 30 and 47 new stores in FY2023 and FY2024, respectively. These improved growth prospects resulted in a higher valuation, the stock is now trading around a 23 times FWD P/E ratio, which could be reasonable but not cheap for a (strong) retailer.

Looking more long-term, I can provide some closing thoughts from this trends report from McKinsey. The beauty market is very healthy and has a long runway. Among other trends, male beauty is expanding in Gen Z and presents opportunity. This could double the market for ULTA beauty, which still caters to women mostly. However, we will have to see how this develops long-term.

Risks

The tariffs provide a risk for retailers importing goods from abroad. However, ULTA is relatively insulated from this risk as it does not directly import goods from China (only 1% of shipments). That being said, increased inflation would hurt discretionary consumption. We will have to see how this will develop.

As with every international expansion, the execution of the strategy could not succeed in all regions. ULTA is still in a testing phase across different markets. Having a strong balance sheet means that ULTA can make these investments and failure will not be catastrophic. The investments are relatively small and low risk, but they could provide substantial upside through improved growth.

Conclusions

While the last year was relatively disappointing, ULTA has beat expectations. My original thesis seems to be correct, in the sense that ULTA has managed to re-accelerate growth; starting its new chapter as an international company.

Last year provided a good buying opportunity at or below $400 per share, but the stock has risen again. Some of the upside has already been captured in the current stock price. That being said, ULTA still provides reasonable value at these prices for a strong company with good growth prospects. Long-term value investors who invested back in 2023 could take some profits.

data.title: Infosys Q2 Preview: Muted Expectations While Waiting For An AI Demand Wave
data.description:
In the run-up to the second quarter earnings season, Indian IT major's stock prices are pointing toward a muted outlook, with the Nifty IT index down 7-8% in the last 3 weeks. One of the major Indian IT companies, Infosys (NYSE:INFY), has seen a similar correction, as the company is set to report its 2QFY26 results on the 16th of October with muted expectations primarily driven by macroeconomic headwinds and recent geopolitical instability between India and the US.

I believe the IT services industry is currently at a pivotal juncture, which is marked by slower IT spending by enterprises and a potential AI-driven demand surge. This makes the next few quarters and commentaries regarding the end market coming out from the major IT players crucial, as this is when the market could turn around.

INFY, being a major IT player with significant investments in AI-related services, should be a potential major beneficiary of this AI demand surge. While the current stock price offers decent valuation, I believe waiting for minor signs of recovery would be beneficial before getting in the stock. Hence, I have a hold (neutral) rating for the time being.

2QFY26 preview: Muted growth and cautious optimism

INFY started the year on an upbeat note, with 1QFY26 beating muted expectations and management raising the lower end of the full-year revenue growth guidance. Back then there was some kind of uncertainty regarding the tariff issue that was causing delay in discretionary IT spending.

As the company transitioned into its second quarter (2QFY26), the acute tariff-related anxiety somewhat stabilized, but now new concerns regarding geopolitical instability are emerging. The fundamental client behavior has seen little change between the two quarters; the "wait and watch" approach continues, as highlighted by the Accenture (ACN) management in their recent earnings call.

Moreover, the ACN's management also retained a neutral stance while giving guidance by considering both macro deterioration and continuing a stable environment in macroeconomic conditions without pointing toward an improving outlook. I believe INFY's 2QFY26 commentary regarding the macro outlook should be in line with what Accenture has recently reported.

Given this backdrop of stability without significant improvement, I believe the 2QFY26 results will not present any meaningful deviations on either the topline or the margin front. Consequently, it is highly probable that INFY will maintain its full-year constant currency [CC] revenue growth guidance of 1% to 3%.

Geographically the European market should continue to perform well owing to recovery in spending from prior lows, while the US market should remain a drag on the overall company given the macroeconomic uncertainty leading the clients to hold on to discretionary spending.

While I do expect a muted outlook from INFY's earnings report, I would be keenly focused on management's commentary regarding subtle shifts in tone regarding potential recovery or any sort of positive developments.

Another important leading indicator to watch out for is the hiring trend, which has been quite muted for the last few quarters. While the management is committed to hiring 20k employees in FY26, the first quarter's lackluster hiring did point toward a cautious outlook by the management. Given the macro backdrop stabilizing now, one should keep an eye on if INFY keeps up with their provided guidance or not. Further, a ramp-up in hiring beyond initial guidance can point toward an improving market condition.

Potential AI spending wave could be around the corner

While things are tough now, INFY is trying to be a key beneficiary of the next big tech cycle driven by AI. The company has invested heavily in building its comprehensive AI suite (Infosys Topaz) and is continuously upskilling and reskilling its talent pool for complex AI-related projects.

While the current landscape is not yet ripe enough to reflect the true potential of AI-related demand, a future surge in AI spending is seen as inevitable. Historically, IT services spending has lagged behind the maturation of new product technologies. Clients tend to be skeptical of adopting a technology in its early stages when it is maturing rapidly, running the risk of their investments soon becoming obsolete if the underlying tech continues to deliver exponential improvements.

The floodgates of enterprise spending typically open once these exponential improvements convert into more predictable, incremental gains, as clients no longer run the risk of their investments becoming obsolete early and can realize the return on their IT spending.

I believe the recent slowdown in performance improvement from newer models like GPT-5 as compared to the huge leap taken by the older generation could be a sign that we are approaching a performance plateau. While performance gains can still be significant (like in the recent Claude Sonnet 4.5), they are not always the groundbreaking leaps seen in 2023-2024.

I believe that as more new-generation models with less dramatic performance gains are released, enterprises will become increasingly keen to deploy large-scale, durable AI-related investments, unlocking the next major wave of growth for which INFY is preparing.

Geopolitical Risk a major concern

The US is by far the largest market of INFY with over 60% of revenue coming from this single country. With the recent tussle between the Indian and US administrations over Russian crude oil purchases and trade deal negotiations, the Indian IT sector has started to witness some heat. In September, the US administration announced a $100k one-time fee for H1B visa applications aimed at creating a barrier for companies using foreign talent.

The Indian IT companies like INFY use this program to send local employees from India to the US for more frontend/client engagement roles. While the move would have limited impact on the company given the limited number of employees working in the US on H1B visas, just 2004 out of the total workforce of 323K employees.

While the H1B move is not a major risk for INFY, the primary concern is the protectionist approach of the US administration. The proposed Halting International Relocation of Employment (HIRE) act further confirms this protectionist stance with respect to foreign talents. This act aims to put a 25% tax on payments made by American companies for outsourced work, which is the primary business of IT services firms like INFY. While the act has a very slim chance of becoming a law given operational and political hurdles this protectionist stance could lead to more such initiatives by the current administration.

Valuation and conclusion

INFY currently trades at a 20.7x forward non-GAAP PE ratio, which is lower than the sector median of 25.3x and its own 5-year average of 26.7x. However, if we widen the horizon and take a decadal median, the company seems to be fairly valued.

While the company has major long-term opportunity, still the near-term uncertainty would keep the stock under pressure until signs of growth recovery emerge. Additionally, there are heightened geopolitical risks, and while recent steps would not have a material impact, the direction toward protectionism is a headwind for IT services businesses like INFY. Valuations do seem reasonable but I would wait for the situation to improve before issuing a buy rating. Until then, I have a neutral [HOLD] rating on the stock.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 10/10/25
data.description:
2This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: ARK Innovation ETF: Time To Go All In
data.description:
Here's a hot take for the day: buy a fund that, despite a poor five-year track record, down 9% cumulatively against the S&P 500's (SPY) 96% gains, has rallied to the stratosphere in the recent past. Oh, on a day that the ETF gained more than 3% in a single trading session.

This is my current position on ARK Innovation (BATS:ARKK). Cathie Wood's flagship ETF has climbed 111% in the past six months alone (see chart below). Such performance has not only put the high-flying Nasdaq 100 (QQQ) to shame, but it has also raised "overbought" red flags that have recently spooked most analysts on Seeking Alpha and other investors.

What I like most about ARKK

ARKK is exposed to the themes that I believe will create substantial value over the very long run, which I will define here as 20 years at least. These themes include electric and autonomous vehicles, represented by ARKK's largest holding, Tesla (TSLA); digital currency, expressed by Coinbase (COIN) at 6% portfolio allocation as of early October; and gaming and media, among others.

I continue to believe that, if given a very long investment horizon (say, my toddler's retirement portfolio), I would much rather buy and hold equity in the leaders of these technologies than low-valuation stocks in old-economy companies, including those in the O&G and industrial spaces, for example.

Yes, ARKK's top holdings are pricey, and have become much more so in the past half year. Among the top five holdings, which account for more than one-third of the portfolio's investments, three are rated F (the lowest rating) by Seeking Alpha on a forward P/E basis; and the other two, CRISPR Therapeutics (CRSP) and Tempus AI (TEM), do not even have a projected positive EPS in the next couple of years to earn the right to have a P/E.

But to me, relative valuation does not matter as much in this case. By default, standard metrics like next-year P/E capture expected short-term performance, which does not match the long-term duration of ARKK's investments.

As a quick example, Shopify (SHOP), ARKK's sixth-largest holding, may trade at an aggressive 2025 EPS multiple of 112x today. But if the consensus is correct about long-term EPS growth rates at 30% (see below), the stock at its current price would trade at a P/E of only 8.2x in ten years. Either the company will eventually disappoint the market on bottom-line growth (certainly possible), or the share price will likely triple or more in the next decade to maintain a modest P/E of 25x in 2035, which would be much more reasonable for this stock.

What I dislike most about ARKK

If valuation (or recent share price outperformance) does not send me running away from ARKK, the fund's discretionary approach to investing makes me a bit hesitant. At a high level, the ETF focuses its investments on disruptive innovation. But how it executes the strategy is largely dependent upon ARK's managers and their team's quantitative and qualitative assessment.

The quasi-black box model impacts my ability to understand ARKK's future holdings, let alone estimate the performance of the fund. What makes it worse, in my view, is the fund manager's track record of selling high-flyers and buying dips, which effectively means that the relative losers remain (or gain prominence) in the portfolio, while the relative winners eventually take a back seat. I do not think that this is the best approach to investing in valuation-insensitive stocks like the ones that usually find their way into the ARKK portfolio (see my argument above on relative valuation).

Management fees, at a sizable 75 bps, could be cited as another weakness for this ETF. However, I do not think that less than one percent in drag to the ETF's annual returns will matter much in the long run, if or when it can perform substantially better (i.e., not by a few bps, but by a handful of percentage points) than the S&P 500.

How I would invest in ARKK today

If I had to rate ARKK from zero to ten on a fundamental criteria scale, I would probably choose a six: I am neither a fanboy nor a hater, although I lean in favor of investing in disruptive innovation.

What makes me much more bullish about owning ARKK today is the overlaying of technical markers, namely moving averages. ARKK has behaved like a bubble that inflated and then burst over the past ten years - and the ETF has finally started to recover again. I spoke at length about how to own and then dispose of potential bubble stocks and ETFs in the past, and ARKK has been one of the prime examples.

In my January 2022 article, I compared ARKK with the Nasdaq 100 during the dot-com event. I warned against owning ARKK at that time, the same way that owning the tech-rich index in December 2000, after an initial large dip that drew in bargain hunters, would have preceded a two-year streak of sizable, double-digit losses in 2001 and 2002.

To own a bubble confidently, I suggest selling the asset once the 50-day moving average dips below the 200-day moving average. This means that an investor can ride the bullish wave for as long as it lasts, but step aside when the tide begins to turn. Below is how this simple moving average strategy would have performed in the case of ARKK since the start of the decade: 19% annual returns vs. buy-and-hold's 12%, with a maximum drawdown of "only" 48% vs. buy-and-hold's 81% peak-to-trough decline.

Therefore, I think that now is a good time to own ARKK, despite the recent run. The ETF remains in a 40%-plus drawdown vs. peak 2021 levels, and it is still underwater over the past five years. The trick, however, is that I would monitor price action daily, as described above, to pull the plug on the investment should the recent doubling of the stock in a mere six months eventually prove to be ARKK's bubble 2.0.

data.title: HP: Stuck, But Still A Value Investor's Play
data.description:
Hewlett-Packard (NYSE:HPQ) remains a classic value investing opportunity these days, aimed for the long haul, highlighted by (1) high operational efficiency with consistently high returns on capital over the years; and (2) a relatively attractive valuation, reflecting a potentially not so well understood (in my opinion) defensive moat.

While in theory, HP is the kind of company that seems to do everything right behind the scenes, the problem is that it hasn't been enough when it needs to deliver to a market hungry for flash and hype. Slow, consistent, and predictable growth is naturally something largely ignored in bull market cycles. To make matters worse, around two-thirds of HP's profits still come from printing, a business that is more like a museum than a digital future.

So, in the short term, that weighs. In fact, based on historical returns, these factors have been weighted for quite some time. Growth investors look at HP and see little excitement with hopes of mild growth in a cyclical segment (PC), and a cash flow that looks tight and could potentially limit buybacks and dividends.

I wouldn't anticipate any short-term turnaround until HP signals to the market that it can truly grow outside of Printing, and generate consistent cash flow and allocate capital intelligently—whether with share buybacks, dividends, or new investments in PCs and corporate services.

However, trading at 8.6x forward P/E, practically in line with its historical average, I still think the HPQ thesis is quite de-risked for the long-term, and ticks many of the boxes of the value investing philosophy.

The proven resilience of HP's business tends to show its worth when the market cools. And so perhaps HPQ's main appeal today is not that it will explode in value during a crisis, but that it will probably suffer less than highly leveraged or hype-dependent growth companies, offering a kind of moderate hedge within the technology/electronics sector.

HP: A Model Long-Term Value Investment

Through the lens of value investing, Hewlett-Packard is a clear standout candidate for investors who prioritize names with high operational efficiency and attractive relative valuations.

To better demonstrate these attributes, Joel Greenblatt's methodology, which combines Return on Capital (ROC)—which basically measures the company's ability to generate profit from its invested capital—together with earnings yield (in the formula of operating profit "EBIT"/enterprise value), shows the investor's implicit return if the entire company were to be acquired.

As the table below shows, the ROC, calculated from EBIT divided by the sum of net working capital (NWC) plus net fixed assets (NFA), has consistently remained above 30% for most of the past decade. HP reached peak years between 2018 and 2021 (with an extremely high ROC >50%), and currently, in the last twelve months, an ROC of 28.3%, which is still solid, slightly lower than peak years.

In super-practical terms, an ROC above (or close to) 30% means that for every dollar HP invests in its business, it generates 30 cents in operating profit. And arguably, it makes much more sense for an investor to allocate their capital into HP, knowing that they can very likely turn it into a high percentage of profit. Although for some this may not be so impressive versus other high-growth tech peers, it is important to note the consistency here, as over the last decade, the average ROC would be 36%. Very few companies achieve this level of consistency, to be clear.

Furthermore, still using Greenblatt's methodology, there would be little point in this high return on capital in isolation if the investor's implicit return on the total price of the company were not attractive. This is why, using the Earnings Yield (EBIT/Enterprise Value) metric, it is possible to accurately measure HPQ valuations.

According to the chart below, over the last decade, HPQ has consistently maintained an EY of between 10% and 16%. If we consider that, based on current long-term bond rates, equity market risk premium, and adjusted beta, a cost of equity for HPQ today would be in the range of ~12.6%. Putting tax rate, debt/equity ratio, and cost of debt together, we could stipulate an after-tax WACC of ~10.2%.

As a rule of thumb, a return above the cost of capital invested implies the creation of real economic value for all financiers. Although the spread is relatively short between WACC and EY for HP, it can still be said that it continues to generate positive economic value, combining not only constant operational efficiency but also an attractive valuation.

As we can see over the last decade, precisely in periods when HP's ROC and EY were at their highest, its returns in the following years were much higher than the broader market.

Theoretically, companies with a very robust and consistent return on capital tend to grow sustainably over the long term.

And normally, companies that manage to do this consistently have a solid moat. In fact, I would dare to say that HP's moat is much more operational/defensive than technological. After all, the company has consolidated global operations in hardware, printers, PCs, and corporate services, which generates efficiencies of scale—something that allows for higher margins and consequently sustains a high ROC.

Probably because it doesn't have more of a technological moat, this is the best explanation for a high earnings yield over the past few years, with the market potentially not giving due credit to an ultra-efficient operating company.

But anyway, the moral of the story is simple: companies, like HP, that manage to reinvest profits with a high ROC should naturally perform better than companies with a low ROC.

HP in the Short-to-Mid Term

For short-term investors, key growth trends have recently dictated HP’s stock performance.

For example, in Fiscal Q3, HP saw a modest 3.1% YoY growth in revenues, slightly beating pessimistic expectations and virtually tied with CPI inflation.

The mix has been made up of tailwinds from the Personal Systems "PC division" (growing 6% YoY in revenues), mainly driven by replacement cycles of old PCs given the demand for AI-ready models, and headwinds from the Printing division, which is facing secular decline (falling 4% YoY in FQ3).

So, I would say here that HPQ has faced a mixed growth environment. But there are warning signs on the horizon. Two-thirds of operating profit (pro ROC basis) comes from the Printing segment, which is going in the complete opposite direction of automation and AI. There are also fears of slowing PC cycles with the current peak linked to the end of Windows 10 and a still reticent consumer spending going forward.

In the last two quarters of fiscal 2025, the impact of tariffs, mainly in the Personal Systems (PCs) segment, forced analysts to revise their 2025 EPS projections downwards by up to 12% from what was estimated six months ago. At similar levels, long-term guidance was revised downwards, capitalizing on further pain ahead.

In order to mitigate such headwinds, HP has accelerated the transfer of its manufacturing outside of China, seeking to establish manufacturing hubs in countries such as Vietnam, India, Mexico, and also in the U.S. The goal is to have more than 90% of the products sold in North America manufactured outside of China. In addition, HP is also seeking a restructuring that would involve cutting $300 million by the end of the current fiscal year (which ended in October), including cutting up to 2,000 jobs.

While layoffs are rarely something to be applauded, I believe these are strategies that demonstrate the necessary frugality at a time of adversity, which rightly brings HP back in line with its operating leverage and historically high returns on capital.

That said, HP comes relatively unimpressed to report its October quarter (for Fiscal Q4), expected to deliver $0.92 in EPS (down 0.8% YoY) and revenues of $14.4 billion (up 2.7% YoY).

I would keep my eyes open for free cash flow trends. In Fiscal Q3, HP showed a downward trend versus 2024, with the gap between FCF vs. operating cash flow widening, corroborating CapEx/margin pressure. With buybacks being cut, given the shorter financial slack, by year-to-date 2024, HP had returned $2.01 billion to shareholders, which was virtually all of its FCF.

In year-to-date 2025, it returned much less ($1.17 billion), suggesting that, although cash generation remains robust, much of the FCF has been used for structural maintenance rather than growth.

The continued high dependence on the Printing segment brings a greater risk of margin compression in the medium term. Therefore, if there is an expansion of EBIT margins, or if there is a smaller gap between OCF and FCF—eventually from a lighter or more efficient CapEx—a greater volume in buybacks would be very welcome and potentially decisive for the resumption of confidence in a short- to medium-term turnaround.

Risks and Final Remarks

HP doesn't have the burden of a high valuation to need to "overdeliver" like other tech names trading at explosive multiples do for a barely positive reaction. But neither does a low valuation signal that it can't go even lower.

To the extent that the Printing segment's dependence on the bottom line continues to be very relevant, the portent that margins will continue to be under pressure and in recession may be something that lasts for a long time, and not even very high and consistent returns on capital can seduce the market enough to generate returns above the benchmark.

On the other hand, arguably, we can say that, even with the slow growth, the pace has been at least enough to keep the operational/defensive moat still consistent for the long term, and the time to invest in value investing picks is precisely when the market is largely overlooking them.

For this reason, I believe that now may be a good time to own small amounts of HPQ, assuming that the horizon is based on the long term. It's difficult to time the market, or to know whether FQ4 or Fiscal 2026 will actually be the inflection point in the thesis. But with shares down ~15% year-to-date, punished by bearish reviews around negative sentiment in terms of consumer softness and tariffs inflating cost structure. Despite this, I remain confident that HP’s consistency could eventually generate gains for patient investors.

data.title: BITQ: Strong Momentum For Crypto Names Into The Year End
data.description:
Thesis

Similar to gold miners rallying in 2025, bitcoin miners are having an astounding year as the currency rallies. Bitwise Crypto Industry Innovators ETF (NYSEARCA:BITQ) is an equities exchange-traded fund that provides focused exposure to the crypto economy without the complications of holding crypto assets outright. The ETF holds a mix of crypto miners, crypto trading platforms, asset management platforms, and infrastructure companies.

The ETF is market weighted and contains 30 names. As per its fact sheet, most of its holdings are mid-cap companies, which make up 56% of the holdings. Large-caps are 25%, while mega-caps represent 16%.

The article will explore some of the holdings in detail, and outline why this ETF is a good momentum play given its very high beta and cyclical nature.

Composition - only equities, no outright cryptocurrencies

The fund segregates its holdings in larger sub-sectors:

  • Mining: 37%
  • Trading & Custody: 35%
  • Treasury Holdings: 15%
  • Asset management: 7.5%
  • Infrastructure: 5.5%

Let us have a look at the top names in the ETF:

The top ten names make up over 63% of the fund, and they represent well-known names in the space. Let us have a closer look at some of them:

1) IREN Ltd (IREN): IREN is an Australia-based company that owns and operates data centers powered by 100% renewable energy for high-performance computing (HPC) applications.

The company has positive earnings, a forward EPS of 0.98, and a 61x P/E ratio. The name trades at 8x book value, but its forward earnings projections are muddled as per Seeking Alpha (i.e. does not show a clear upwards trajectory). As per Seeking Alpha, the average price target as per Wall St analysts is $48 per share.

2) MARA Holdings (MARA): Mara is an American digital asset technology and cryptocurrency mining company. The name has several business segments: bitcoin mining, digital asset treasury, and energy and infrastructure. In terms of bitcoin mining, MARA operates specialized computers to validate transactions on the Bitcoin blockchain and earn Bitcoin. Thus the higher the price of Bitcoin, the higher the NAV for Mara. The treasury side involves holding strategic Bitcoin reserves for long term gains.

The company has positive earnings, a forward EPS of 0.48, and a 41x P/E ratio. The name trades at 1.5x book value, and its forward earnings projections are also muddled with negative EPS projected for the future. The average price target as per Wall St analysts is $23 per share.

3) Riot Platforms (RIOT): Riot is a publicly traded, vertically integrated Bitcoin mining company and infrastructure platform. The name operates several business segments, including Bitcoin mining, treasury services, and an AI computing platform.

The company has negative earnings estimates for both 2025 and 2026, although it has posted quarters of positive earnings in the past. Its operating income until 2024 has always been negative. The company trades at 2.4x book value, and the average price target per Wall St analysts is $21 per share.

4) Bitdeer Technologies Group (BTDR): Although not in the top 10, the fund does hold a 2% allocation to BTDR. Bitdeer is a global technology company focused on providing a comprehensive suite of computing solutions for the blockchain and high-performance computing industries. BTDR operates several segments, such as bitcoin mining, cloud hash rate, and infrastructure.

The company has negative earnings estimates for 2025, but a 0.91 EPS estimate for 2026. Furthermore, EPS is seen doubling in 2027. The name trades at 6x book value, and the average Wall St. estimate is $25/share.

A very cyclical ETF

Let us start by looking at some of the fund's analytics:

  • AUM: $0.5 billion
  • Expense ratio: 0.85%
  • St deviation (3Y): 67%
  • Beta: 2.08

As we can see this name is not for the faint of heart, with a 67% standard deviation, and a drawdown profile that looks like this:

Yes, you are reading the above chart correctly, where you see the ETF with a -90% drawdown in late 2022. It has not yet recovered its entire drawdown since inception, but the performance this year has been spectacular, with the fund up +71%. The performance per year is as follows as per Morningstar:

  • 2022: -83%
  • 2023: +246%
  • 2024: +47%
  • YTD: +71%

We can see the pattern here, with risk-off years having massive drawdowns, while risk-on years see substantial gains. This ETF is not a buy and hold in our view, but a cyclical play after a recession. We can also compare the ETF versus the bitcoin performance for the respective years:

  • 2022: -64%
  • 2023: +156%
  • 2024: +121%
  • YTD: +31%

When we compare the two sets of data, we can see BITQ is a high-beta play on bitcoin, with larger drawdowns but also much larger positive recoveries.

A momentum play into the year end

As we have articulated above, BITQ is not a buy and hold, but a cyclical momentum play. Crypto has been very attractive in 2025 given deficit concerns in the U.S. and the dollar depreciation. A strong bitcoin has percolated into the crypto universe, with ample moves for the miners and infrastructure companies. BITQ is exhibiting strong momentum as per Seeking Alpha, with an A+ momentum grade and a 'Strong Buy' quant rating.

The current government shutdown has also put upward momentum on crypto, with investors moving to an asset class that is not affected by bipartisan politics. We are of the opinion that the crypto universe will continue to deliver for the rest of 2025, with strong momentum and a high beta.

However be careful with BITQ by picking an entry and exit point. This is not an ETF you want to hold long term. Our opinion is around strong momentum into December; thus, the penciled exit point is at the end of 2025.

Conclusion

BITQ is an equities ETF. The fund aggregates crypto miners, crypto trading platforms and crypto infrastructure companies. Many underlying holdings have negative EPS and very high P/E ratios, but are set to grow if bitcoin prices remain high. The fund is a high beta play on crypto prices and exhibits a high cyclicality. We like this ETF as a momentum play into the year end, but be mindful because BITQ is not a buy and hold by any means.

data.title: Public Storage: Solid Cash Flow But Not A Buy Right Now
data.description:
The REIT class alone is not the most exciting in the world. It is generally for those looking for something more stable, without many surprises, but even so, within this world it is possible to find sectors such as logistics, data centers, or science, which have many more prospects and exciting aspects to analyze.

This is not quite the case with self-storage. It is not a sector that will revolutionize any trend, nor is it a REIT with several partners listed on the stock exchange. In fact, it is one of the simplest businesses there is: the company owns buildings and rents out space to people who need it.

At the top of this simplicity is Public Storage (NYSE:PSA), which, despite being a little boring, is very profitable and perhaps a little overvalued, which is why my rating is a “solid hold” rather than a buy.

Public Storage: Many Solid Points In This Investment Case

Simplicity can sometimes be linked to a lack of moats, but that is not quite the case with Public Storage. The company has several competitive edges that are important to mention.

The most obvious is scale. The company has more than 3,000 properties spread across the US, so it ends up building a brand and being a default choice for many customers. This is pure scale, as illustrated by its enterprise value of $65 billion, which places it among the largest REITs when we look at this KPI, and also makes it more than $20 billion larger than its main peer, Extra Space Storage (EXR).

Being much larger than its main peers, and still being in this more fragmented market, adds many practical advantages, not only in terms of diluting costs and expenses such as marketing, but also in terms of access to cheaper capital for expansion, the ability to grow through acquisitions, and so on.

Another important point to mention is the operational aspect of this REIT. PSA has high margins, and in principle, this can also be attributed to the business model, but credit must also be given to management's execution. As mentioned in the earnings call, the company is “the most efficient operating platform” in the industry. This is the result of initiatives such as investment in digital, customer experience, and well-executed acquisitions, as mentioned in the excerpt below:

“Public Storage's industry leadership is proven by, among other things, the highest revenue generation per square foot among peers, the most efficient operating platform, including customer and employee-centric technologies that are enhancing satisfaction while bolstering our revenue and margin advantages and the strongest ability to drive portfolio expansion through our best-in-class acquisition and development teams backed by our growth-oriented balance sheet. As the operating environment stabilizes, new competitive supply deliveries declined further and the transaction market becomes more active, we are holistically enhancing our advantages and positioning for growth.”

Finally, it is worth mentioning the balance sheet, which stands out when compared to the rest of the REIT industry. Debt to EBITDA ended at 3.1x in Q2, a very healthy level, especially when considering PSA's cash flow generation capacity.

Q2 Earnings And Value Creation

Q2 earnings were not that great, with super-accelerated growth or anything like that. But it serves to illustrate what I mentioned at the beginning of the analysis. Core FFO per share was $4.28, a small YoY increase of just over 1%, but with positive same-store revenue growth.

And the value creation engine continues to work, mainly through acquisitions and expansion. The 2020 properties, for example, already generate a yield of 12.4% (although growth has not been that interesting). This also makes me think that there is still a lot of room for growth through the maturation of the newer part of the portfolio, as the chart below suggests.

PSA Valuation: That’s Why It’s a Hold

The reason I don't give PSA a buy rating is precisely because of its valuation. It's not a particularly overvalued company, but it's not attractively priced either.

The price-to-FFO is 17.5x, which for a REIT that is not growing so rapidly is not that appealing. Compared to its peers (and other REIT segments), it is also not a multiple that allows for much upside and a high dividend yield. For instance, CubeSmart (CUBE) and National Storage Affiliates (NSA) trade at 15.4x and 13.8x, respectively. All of its peers also offer a higher forward dividend yield than PSA.

This is justified by the quality of the company, but in my view, it is too high a premium to pay at the moment.

The Bottom Line

PSA's execution is excellent, its business model is phenomenal, which makes its cash flow prospects and, consequently, its dividends very reliable.

At the same time, the valuation does not allow me to be so optimistic, mainly when we’re seeing so many quality REITs like VICI Properties (VICI) and Realty Income (O) trading at 12x - 13x FFO. Therefore, I prefer to leave the rating as a “solid hold” for now.

data.title: Gold Slips Below $4,000 As Dollar Strength And Easing Tensions Spark Profit-Taking
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

Gold prices retreated on Thursday, dipping below the key $4,000 level after a sharp rally that pushed the metal to multi-year highs earlier this week. The pullback came as traders locked in profits and a rebound in the U.S. dollar index weighed on bullion demand.

The dollar gained 0.5% to reach a two-month high, making gold more expensive for foreign investors and temporarily halting its upward momentum. The easing of geopolitical tensions in the Middle East also reduced safe-haven inflows that had fueled the recent surge.

Technically, gold has encountered resistance near the upper boundary of its rising channel and is now consolidating toward the midline around $3,950. The broader structure, however, remains constructive.

Since late summer, gold has held firmly within an ascending channel pattern, with consistent higher lows supported by the 20-day and 50-day exponential moving averages (EMAs), now positioned at $3,988 and $3,926, respectively. These levels form a critical support zone that will determine whether the current pullback is a temporary correction or the start of a deeper retracement.

Technical cooling offers space for consolidation

Momentum indicators reflect a cooling of the earlier rally. The Relative Strength Index (RSI) has declined from overbought territory above 70 to around 46, indicating a reset in short-term momentum.

This decline suggests the market is working off its stretched conditions rather than signaling a breakdown. Analysts note that such cooling phases often precede renewed buying interest, particularly within a strong uptrend.

If prices fail to hold above the $3,950–$3,920 region, the next area of interest lies near $3,840, which aligns with the lower band of the channel. A steeper decline could see gold test $3,720, though this remains a less likely scenario given the continued strength in macro fundamentals.

On the upside, a rebound above $4,050 would reinforce bullish control and set the stage for a retest of $4,100 and potentially fresh record highs if global risk appetite weakens again.

Broader fundamentals remain supportive

Despite short-term volatility, gold’s longer-term outlook remains positive. The metal is up over 50% year-to-date, supported by sustained central bank purchases and persistent ETF inflows as investors hedge against macroeconomic uncertainty.

Expectations of further Federal Reserve rate cuts in the coming months add to gold’s appeal, as lower interest rates reduce the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding assets.

Geopolitical dynamics also continue to underpin gold’s safe-haven demand. While tensions have temporarily eased, market participants remain cautious amid unresolved fiscal challenges in the U.S. and global growth concerns.

Analysts suggest that any renewed escalation in risk sentiment could quickly restore buying momentum, particularly as structural demand from sovereign reserves and institutional hedgers remains robust.

Outlook

In the near term, the $3,950–$3,920 zone serves as the key battleground for gold’s next move. A successful defense of this level could attract renewed buying interest and keep the broader uptrend intact.

Conversely, a decisive break lower could trigger a correction toward $3,840 before buyers return. For now, the metal’s long-term bias remains bullish, supported by favorable macro conditions and ongoing demand from both retail and institutional investors.

Earlier analysis highlighted that gold’s climb above $4,000 signaled strong momentum but warned that overbought conditions could trigger a healthy retracement. That scenario has now materialized, with the pullback aligning with profit-taking rather than a structural reversal.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: CorMedix: Challenging Long-Term Risk-Reward, Maintain Sell
data.description:
Introduction

CorMedix's stock (NASDAQ:CRMD) is up 59% since my Sell, rating in late March.

Despite progress, such as the company achieving its first profitable quarter, I was cautious about CorMedix's long-term prospects. Moreover, my discounted cash flow (DCF) model implied that CRMD was priced for ~20% Y/Y revenue growth and ~18% free cash flow (FCF) margins, which seemed unrealistic over a nine-year projection for a company that markets a catheter lock solution.

The following article explores what went wrong in my last analysis and CorMedix’s future in light of recent developments (e.g., DefenCath performance, Melinta acquisition).

Recent Developments

I'll admit it: CorMedix is off to a blistering start in 2025. There's no denying it. DefenCath's net revenue was $39.1 million in Q1 and $39.7 million in Q2. Both quarters were profitable, with net income of $20.6 million and $19.8 million in Q1 and Q2, respectively.

However, let’s take a step back. As implied by the company’s original Q2 estimate range and the actual result, DefenCath remains challenging to model. The beat versus the initial midpoint ($39.7M vs. $26.9M) was +47.6%. The beat versus the initial upper bound was +28.5%. Of course, I am not trying to argue that a beat is a “bad thing.” Rather, I am pointing out that investors should not “overfit” to these results.

What I got wrong in March was the near-term trajectory. 1H25 sell-in and LDO rollout were stronger and more durable than I (or management) anticipated. Moreover, limited visibility (recall that management had only guided for 1H) did not translate into an immediate fade. Lastly, the company has since diversified its offerings (reducing “single-asset” exposure) following the Melinta acquisition (more on this later).

DefenCath Structural Risks Remain Despite Strong Sell-In

That being said, core DefenCath risks remain (think inventory timing, customer concentration, price erosion, and reimbursement clocks). Moreover, an inherently onboarding-driven launch can swing in both directions. All in all, while the execution was there in 1H, durability remains a major unknown.

As for FY25 DefenCath revenue, management now expects revenue between $200 million and $250 million, with upside potential on LDO progress.

Looking ahead, we now have a fixed TDAPA window. CMS explicitly lists DefenCath (HCPCS J0911) as TDAPA-eligible from July 1, 2024, through June 30, 2026 (2 years).

Why is this important? I argue that TDAPA expiration will not only impact price, but could impact adoption and sell-in as well.

For example, assume 30 vials/month and $200 net/vial (DefenCath WAC is $249.99 per 3 mL, but net realized price varies due to rebates, discounts, etc.). Post-TDAPA, the price may drop 20% (revenue will also drop 20%). Also, if adoption among CVC patients drops from, say, 60% to 40% (lower utilization incentive post-TDAPA), revenue drops 33%. If you combine both, that results in a ~47% revenue decline on the outpatient book.

More on TDAPA's impact on adoption: There is precedent for products seeing decreased adoption post-TDAPA. For instance, in 2021, etelcalcetide (prescribed to hemodialysis patients to prevent complications of elevated parathyroid hormone) use "decreased substantially after TDAPA ended in January 2021, with most patients switching to cinacalcet." This was even despite etelcalcetide being associated with better outcomes. While DefenCath and etelcalcetide are different drug classes (systemic calcimimetic vs. catheter lock), the economic incentives are analogous. In fact, the authors concluded that:

These findings have US policy implications not only for etelcalcetide and parathyroid hormone but also for future development of innovative therapies in dialysis.

Of course, there are alternatives to DefenCath (e.g., heparin-only locks, citrate locks, ethanol locks, compounded taurolidine locks) and other ways to offset CRBSI risk (e.g., reduce catheter days, access-care bundle, targeted prophylaxis only).

However, the reimbursement clock is not DefenCath’s only risk. Q/Q print volatility will remain, and customer concentration will amplify swings. For instance, in 2024, one customer (presumably an LDO) made up 86% of total revenue.

Another consideration, especially with TDAPA, is that LDOs face reduced expiry risk when stocking DefenCath. This is because DefenCath "customers" can return the product near/past expiry (up to six months) if it's unutilized. So, this materially reduces "expiry risk" for dialysis chains stocking DefenCath. Think about it. If you know you're able to return a product without a problem, you're more incentivized to try and buy it!

Please note that I do not intend to come off as a DefenCath “doomer.” I just want people to understand that the strong 1H sell-in does not automatically equal durable demand. Moreover, with TDAPA ending June 30, 2026, price and utilization could take a hit. So, do not extrapolate Q1 and Q2 figures (net income included).

The Melinta Acquisition

Although pricey at 2.5x sales, CorMedix’s recent $300 million (in upfront consideration, please read PR for terms and financing) acquisition of Melinta Therapeutics effectively diversifies CorMedix’s offerings and smooths Q/Q DefenCath revenue volatility.

The deal gives CorMedix a ready-made hospital/acute-care portfolio, including products like Rezzayo (an anti-fungal), Baxdela (fluroquinolone for ABSSSI), and Toprol-XL (cardiovascular brand), among others. It also has many synergies (think cross-selling and channel leverage), so it makes a lot of sense on paper.

DefenCath’s last two quarters imply a $160 million sell-in annualized run rate. Melinta’s steady and dependable $100-110 million in core product revenue should help cushion DefenCath’s quarterly volatility.

With that said, hospital anti-infectives carry heavy GTN and modest organic growth. So, while CorMedix's revenue base gets an immediate "facelift," I wouldn't expect Melinta to meaningfully accelerate its overall growth.

The company is hopeful that Rezzayo's label can expand to include "prophylaxis of invasive fungal infections in adult patients undergoing allogeneic blood and marrow transplant." Moreover, it is still evaluating DefenCath in patients receiving total parenteral nutrition. CorMedix estimates that both indications (together) could add up to $400 million in peak annual sales.

Before we move on to "valuation assumptions," I have to point out that the acquisition really changes things. Think about it carefully. CorMedix, with one product, did not just acquire another product. It acquired an entire company with its own portfolio of products. CorMedix is transformed, for better or worse. As the deal just recently closed, please consider the inherent uncertainty associated with modeling. Below is my best attempt.

Valuation Assumptions

Now that CorMedix and Melinta are "one and the same," let’s model it (see "Appendix" for input assumptions).

My Monte Carlo simulation, which accounts for variability among key inputs, implies that while CRMD appears “overvalued” by nearly 25%, it is not unreasonably overvalued (the current price falls under my Monte Carlo's 95th percentile price of $11.45).

So, the market has higher expectations of CRMD than I do (in one scenario, 11.8% Y/Y revenue growth and 12% FCF margins result in a 0% difference between DCF value per share and current stock price).

It’s feasible that Melinta-CorMedix synergies could push FCF margins (CRMD’s primary driver) >13%, supporting CorMedix’s current valuation. However, I really feel that 10% Y/Y revenue growth and FCF margins, on average, over nine years are generous for an acute-care focused company. I believe this is a fair “base” scenario.

Final Thoughts

At ~2.7x 2025E EV/S (given the durability and quality of the sales) and a DCF of $8-9/share on conservative post-TDAPA assumptions, additional upside is challenging to envision. From my porch, TDAPA reset, DefenCath customer concentration risks, returns/stocking noise, and Melinta GTN/integration portend unfavorable risk-reward (maintain “Sell”).

Things that could change my mind include a more conservative valuation (<$10/share), CVC adoption sustained post-TDAPA, reorder cadence (returns <3% of gross), $30-45 million in synergies realized, durable hospital contribution (>$6-8 million/quarter), and post-acquisition FCF >12%.

In the near term, should DefenCath sell-in accelerate and Melinta-related synergies exceed expectations, there could be meaningful upside in CorMedix. However, unless I explicitly state otherwise, my analyses are focused on long-term fundamentals.

Appendix

DCF model input assumptions:

  • “Year 1 Revenue” of $335 million is the midpoint of management's guidance after Melinta's close.
  • While “Shares Outstanding” are closer to 78M, I estimated for 81M to allow for deal contingencies.
  • “Debt” is from Melinta’s YE‘24 notes payable.
  • “Cash” of $90 million is a pro-forma estimate due to “balance sheet” uncertainty post-acquisition.
  • “Revenue Growth” of 10% is an assumption that takes into consideration the 2025 pro-forma guide, a 2H26 headwind post-TDAPA, and modest growth thereafter.
  • “FCF Margin” of 10% is an assumption calibrated to acute-care GTN drag, returns/working capital, and the $35 million-$45 million synergy target.
  • Everything else is either standard DCF methodology (e.g., terminal growth % of 2) or was derived from financial statements (e.g., Melinta’s interest expense in 2024 implies ~9% cost of debt).

One last note: Single-point estimates can be inherently misleading. In reality, there are many combinations that can result in “DCF Value per Share = Current Stock Price.”

Per my model, CRMD’s valuation implies any combination of revenue growth (between ~9.5% and 24%) and FCF margins (between 6% and 14%).

As I always say, my primary purpose of DCF modeling is not to determine whether a stock is under- or overvalued; it is to find out what growth and margin assumptions are "baked" in. After uncovering implied assumptions, I can then evaluate whether or not they are realistic.

data.title: Palantir Stock Steadies Near $185 As AI Optimism Offsets Valuation Concerns
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR) is holding steady near $185 after rebounding from its 50-day exponential moving average (EMA), with price action continuing to respect the boundaries of its long-standing ascending channel. Despite several bouts of volatility this year, the stock has maintained its bullish structure, supported by higher lows and consistent institutional buying.

The near-term focus now centers on the $186–$188 resistance area, where a descending dotted trendline has repeatedly stalled advances. A clean breakout above this level could invite renewed momentum toward $195, with the psychological $200 threshold as the next upside target.

The broader technical structure remains favorable as long as Palantir holds above its key moving averages. Immediate support lies near $181, aligned with the 20-day EMA, while the 50- and 100-day EMAs at $176 and $169, respectively, provide deeper cushioning in the event of a correction.

The Relative Strength Index (RSI) at 59 signals a neutral-to-bullish bias, suggesting there is still room for further upside before overbought conditions emerge. A breakdown below $176, however, could shift sentiment short-term and expose the stock to a pullback toward the lower boundary of the channel near $155.

Technical momentum aligns with steady AI optimism

Palantir’s enduring rally has been fueled by sustained enthusiasm surrounding its artificial intelligence platform. The company’s unique ontology-driven data integration and decision intelligence framework have become central to its competitive moat, allowing clients across defense, energy, and enterprise sectors to adopt AI-based solutions at scale.

Recent strength in government contracts, including partnerships with NATO and the U.K. Ministry of Defense, continues to underpin revenue stability in its public sector division.

On the commercial side, Palantir’s AI operating system rollout has been gaining traction among global enterprises. Bank of America recently reaffirmed its confidence in Palantir’s business trajectory by raising its price target to $215, calling the firm one of the most strategically positioned AI companies in the U.S. tech sector. That assessment has reinforced investor conviction even as broader market momentum in AI-related equities shows signs of cooling.

Valuation remains the key restraint

Despite strong business fundamentals, Palantir’s valuation remains a flashpoint for analysts. The company trades at more than 130 times sales, the highest among S&P 500 constituents, highlighting the premium investors have placed on its long-term AI growth narrative.

Even under optimistic projections of $18 billion in annual revenue by 2030, the firm would still command a 24-times sales multiple - well above industry norms. This disconnect between robust operational execution and stretched valuation levels leaves Palantir exposed to profit-taking, particularly if sentiment toward AI equities weakens or macro headwinds intensify.

Still, the stock’s consistent technical resilience and deep institutional interest suggest that the broader uptrend remains intact. Investors appear willing to tolerate premium valuations as long as Palantir continues to deliver growth in government contracts and expand its commercial AI footprint.

Outlook

In the near term, attention will focus on whether Palantir can decisively break above the $188 resistance zone. A successful move higher could trigger a retest of $195 and eventually the $200 mark, while failure to hold above $176 would invite a deeper correction toward $155. The medium-term trend, however, remains positive, supported by ongoing AI adoption and structural demand for data-driven enterprise solutions.

Earlier analysis emphasized that Palantir’s sustained channel pattern and consistent EMA support reinforced its bullish trajectory, provided the stock held above $176. That view remains valid as price action continues to align with its long-term uptrend despite short-term valuation pressures.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post
